title: How to Read LLM Leaderboards
concept: Always check for contamination and evaluate across multiple benchmarks rather than relying on a single leaderboard score. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Hallucination Detection.'

exercises.prompt: How can we accurately judge an AI model's performance on a leaderboard?

options.text: By only looking at the very top ranked model on a single website.
By checking multiple benchmarks and watching out for test contamination.
By picking the model with the coolest sounding name.

exercises.explain: To get a true picture of an AI's skills, we must evaluate it across various tasks and make sure it hasn't seen the test questions beforehand.

cards.title: Is #1 Really the Best?
cards.body: When you see an AI model boasting that it is '#1 on the leaderboard,' should you believe the hype? Not so fast! Just like in video games, high scores on AI boards don't always tell the whole story. Let's learn how to spot the real winners!

cards.title: What is an LLM Leaderboard?
cards.body: An LLM Leaderboard ranks AI models based on how well they perform on standard tests called benchmarks. However, a high rank can be misleading if the model has already 'seen' the test questions during its training (a problem called 'data contamination'), or if it only excels at one specific type of task.

cards.title: The Test Prep Trap
cards.body: Think of a leaderboard like a school report card. If a student only gets tested on the exact questions they practiced at home, they will score a perfect 100%! But that doesn't mean they are a genius. To see how smart they actually are, we need to test them on brand new, unexpected questions from different teachers.

cards.title: Choosing the Right Model
cards.body: Imagine Model A ranks #1 on a math leaderboard, but fails completely at writing stories or coding. Meanwhile, Model B ranks #3 in math, but also ranks very high in logic, translation, and coding. For a general chatbot, Model B is actually the much better choice!

cards.title: The Myth of the Perfect Score
cards.body: People often think that the model at the very top of a leaderboard is the absolute best at everything. In reality, models can 'cheat' by accident if the test questions were part of their training data. Always look for diverse tests to find the true champion!

cards.title: Smart vs. Blind Trust
cards.body: Here is how to compare a misleading score with a reliable evaluation.

left.text: Trusting a single high score on a public test where the AI might have memorized the answers.

right.text: Comparing scores across many different tests and checking for clean, unseen data.

exercises.prompt: Why should you evaluate an AI model across multiple benchmarks instead of just one?

options.text: Because using only one benchmark makes the computer run too hot.
To get a well-rounded view of the model's abilities and avoid biased results.
Because leaderboards only allow you to look at one score per day.
To make sure the model is the most expensive one available.

exercises.explain: Looking at multiple benchmarks gives you a complete picture of an AI's strengths and weaknesses, rather than trusting a single potentially biased score.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI model suffers from data contamination, it will always perform better on new, real-world tasks.
exercises.explain: Data contamination makes a model look better on paper because it already knows the answers, but it fails to generalize to new, real-world tasks.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of why some AI models get fake high scores.
exercises.sentence: When an AI model has already seen the test questions during its training, we call this data contamination.
exercises.explain: Data contamination happens when test questions sneak into the training material, making the AI look smarter on leaderboards than it actually is.

exercises.prompt: Which of these developer statements shows a lack of understanding about how to read LLM leaderboards?

options.text: 'Our model is the absolute best because it got a 100% score on this one math benchmark!'
'We need to test this model on multiple different benchmarks to see its true logic skills.'
'I am worried about data contamination, so let's run our own private test set.'

exercises.explain: The first response is likely AI-generated because it uses suspicious marketing hype ('100% flawless score') and ignores the reality of data contamination, whereas the others acknowledge the need for multiple tests.

exercises.prompt: You are choosing an AI model for your company's new app. What is the safest and most reliable way to evaluate the models using leaderboards?

options.text: Verify performance on multiple benchmarks and check for data contamination.
Only trust the highest score on a single popular leaderboard.
Retrain the model on the leaderboard tests to make it perform better.
Ignore leaderboards entirely and guess which model is best.

exercises.explain: Never trust a single high score! Always look for models that perform well across multiple benchmarks and check if they've cheated by memorizing the test questions. Next up: Hallucination Detection.
